metadata.title: Quick-fire: When the resource itself is fuzzy
metadata.author: patterns-of-choice editorial draft
metadata.design_intent: Companion to qf-allocation-001. Whereas the first round varied counterparty across clean cash-allocation contexts (roommate, friend, stranger, coworker, friend-loan, anonymous tip), this one varies the *shape* of the resource: gift overpayment (windfall), repeat-giving recalibration, asymmetric consumption with equal-split norm, gift sizing by recipient circumstance, named-vs-anonymous donation, family-inheritance redirect. Each item still maps cleanly onto the values-deck (generosity / fairness / need_sensitivity / reciprocity_pure / self_reliance) but the resource itself is shaped differently per item.
metadata.domain_anchor: Dictator-game meta (Engel 2011) for cash-allocation calibration; Aknin 2013 prosocial-spending for the anonymity-of-donation item; Henrich 2020 cross-cultural fairness work for inheritance norm variation.

items.prompt: A relative gives you a wedding gift envelope containing twice the amount you'd expect. You suspect a counting mistake.

options.text: Mention it tactfully when you thank them.
Accept it; they meant it as a gesture and pointing this out diminishes the gift.

items.prompt: An unhoused person you've helped a few times before (small amounts) asks today for $40 — specifically, for a transit pass for a job interview.

options.text: Give the full amount; the stated need is concrete and you can afford it.
Give a smaller amount; the larger ask is a pattern shift worth being cautious about.

items.prompt: Group dinner. The bill is $300; the suggestion is equal-split at $50 each. You ordered the cheapest thing on the menu and didn't drink. Speaking up changes the math by ~$15 in your favor.

options.text: Let the equal split stand; it's the social norm here.
Mention the asymmetry and propose itemizing.

items.prompt: A cousin's birthday. You typically spend $80 on close friends' birthday gifts. Your cousin is in a tight financial spot right now; you suspect a more practical gift at higher value would help materially.

options.text: Stick to the $80 norm; treating them as a charity case would be its own slight.
Spend more, framed as a practical-gift choice rather than charity.

items.prompt: A community fundraiser is $100 short of its goal. You can put your name on the donor list at $100, or donate the $100 anonymously.

options.text: Donate publicly; visible giving signals to others to give too.
Donate anonymously; the giving shouldn't be about you.

items.prompt: A family inheritance has been split equally among siblings. Your share is modest. One sibling is in significantly tighter circumstances than the rest. You could quietly redirect a portion of your share to them.

options.text: Redirect a portion privately; the equal split doesn't reflect actual need.
Keep the split as the family decided; quietly adjusting would create a new asymmetry you'd have to maintain.
